name: Veriff Identity Verification API
what_it_does: Provides video/selfie-based biometric identity verification and document verification for KYC (Know Your Customer) compliance. Agents initiate a verification session via REST API, redirect the user to a hosted Veriff verification flow, then receive a decision via webhook or polling. Returns structured decision objects (approved/declined/resubmission required) with reason codes and extracted document data.
use_cases: Initiate KYC verification sessions for new user onboarding in fintech or crypto exchange applications
Trigger identity re-verification for high-risk transactions detected by a fraud detection agent
Poll or receive webhooks for verification decisions and update user account status accordingly
Extract verified personal data (name, DOB, document number) from approved sessions to pre-fill application forms
Build compliance workflows where agent orchestrates document collection, verification, and approval gating
not_for: Real-time identity checks with sub-second latency — verification decisions can take minutes to hours
Non-KYC authentication use cases (use OAuth/passkeys instead)
Self-service identity verification without a human in the loop on the end-user side — Veriff requires real users to complete the flow
best_when: You need regulated KYC/AML compliance with biometric liveness detection and document verification, and your workflow can accommodate an asynchronous user-facing verification step.
avoid_when: You need instant identity verification or your users cannot complete a selfie/video verification flow on their device.

auth.notes: REST API authenticated with API key and secret via HMAC-SHA256 request signing. Each request requires a generated X-AUTH-CLIENT header and a signature digest. Webhook payloads are signed with HMAC-SHA256 for payload verification. Auth setup is more complex than simple bearer tokens — agents must implement the signing scheme correctly.

pricing.free_tier_limits: No public free tier. Sandbox/test environment available for development.
pricing.paid_tiers: Enterprise pricing only — custom contracts based on verification volume. Typical range $1.50-$5+ per verification. Volume discounts apply.
pricing.notes: Pricing is contract-based and not published publicly. Sales engagement required to get production access. A sandbox environment is provided for integration testing without production contract.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: API errors return structured JSON with code and message fields. Verification decision reasons are documented with reason codes but are sometimes ambiguous (e.g., 'DOCUMENT_DAMAGED' vs 'DOCUMENT_NOT_SUPPORTED'). HMAC auth errors can be opaque — incorrect signature produces a 401 with minimal debug info.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Session creation is not idempotent — each call creates a new session. Agents should store session IDs and check existing sessions before creating new ones to avoid duplicate verification requests for the same user.
known_agent_gotchas: Webhook delivery is not guaranteed to be in order — verification decisions can arrive out of sequence; agents must handle idempotent webhook processing and check session status via polling if a webhook is missed
HMAC request signing requires precise timestamp handling — requests are rejected if the timestamp drift exceeds a few minutes; agents must use NTP-synced clocks
Sessions expire after a configurable window (default 7 days) — agents must track session expiry and re-initiate verification if the user does not complete the flow in time

PII data from verified documents (name, DOB, address) is only available for a limited retention window after decision — agents must fetch and store data promptly or it will be purged per GDPR policies

security_score_components.security_notes: HMAC-SHA256 request signing provides strong authentication — significantly more secure than bare API keys. Webhook payloads are signed for integrity verification. GDPR compliance is core to the product. ISO 27001 and SOC2 certified. Handles extremely sensitive PII — data retention policies must be carefully implemented by integrators.
